notes: Indian restaurant dishes. Restaurants don't publish nutrition facts — values estimated from USDA/recipe databases. Restaurant portions tend to be larger and higher in oil/butter/cream than homemade. Serving sizes reflect typical restaurant portions.

items.notes: The #1 Indian restaurant order. Cream + butter + tomato sauce. Restaurants use 3-4 tbsp butter/cream per serving. Very calorie-dense sauce. FatSecret generic 'chicken curry with cream sauce' ~384 cal/cup but restaurant versions are richer.

Similar to butter chicken but with more tomato and spice. FatSecret verified: 384 cal, 37g protein, 18g carbs, 18g fat per cup — but that's homemade-style. Restaurant adds more oil/cream, so +80-100 cal realistic. Second most popular Indian dish in US.
Richest of the chicken curries — coconut cream, cashew paste, yogurt base. Higher fat than tikka masala. The 'mild' option that's actually the most calorie-dense.
Spicy tomato-vinegar based curry. LEANER than butter chicken/korma — no cream/coconut. The spice lover's better option. ~100 fewer cal than butter chicken.
Spinach-based curry. Better than butter chicken — spinach base means less cream needed. Good fiber from greens. One of the better protein-to-calorie ratios among curries.

THE LEAN OPTION. Yogurt-marinated, clay oven roasted — NO cream sauce. FatSecret shows 519 cal/cup but that's boneless meat packed tight. Standard restaurant half-tandoori is ~300 cal. Best protein-to-calorie ratio on any Indian menu.
Rice cooked with chicken, ghee, saffron. FatSecret verified: 348 cal, 16g protein, 48g carbs, 10g fat per cup. Restaurant serves 2-3 cups = 700-1050 cal per plate. The carb bomb — mostly rice.

Kashmiri lamb in tomato-yogurt sauce with whole spices. Moderate fat from lamb + oil. FatSecret lamb curry base: 257 cal/cup, but rogan josh has more oil in the sauce. Better than korma, worse than vindaloo.
Spicy vinegar-tomato lamb curry. Similar to chicken vindaloo but lamb is fattier. No cream = leaner than korma. The spicy option.
Goat is leaner than lamb. Bone-in pieces mean less edible meat per serving. Simple onion-tomato-spice gravy. Good protein option if you don't mind bones.
Like chicken biryani but with fattier lamb. Restaurant serves 2-3 cups per plate. More calorie-dense than chicken version due to lamb fat rendering into rice.
Chickpeas in tomato-onion-spice gravy. HIGH FIBER (10g/cup) — very satiating. No cream. One of the best vegetarian options for staying full. Moderate calories.
THE LEAN VEG OPTION. Yellow lentils with tempered spices. Low fat, high fiber, high protein for a veg dish. Dal tadka is lighter than dal makhani. Part of the lean Indian order.
Black lentils slow-cooked with butter and cream. 'Makhani' = butter. 120 cal MORE than dal tadka because of butter/cream finish. The rich version of dal.
Paneer (Indian cheese) is calorie-dense: ~300 cal per 100g. Spinach base is healthy but the cheese + oil push it up. Popular veg option but not as lean as people think.
Dry or semi-dry curry. Moderate calories but LOW protein — basically starchy veg in oil. Not a protein source. Better as a side than a main.
Deep-fried paneer/potato balls in rich cream-cashew sauce. The MOST calorie-dense vegetarian dish. Fried kofta + cream sauce = double fat hit. Avoid if cutting.
Mixed vegetables in coconut-cream-cashew sauce. Rich and mild. FatSecret generic vegetable curry: 208 cal/cup, but korma sauce adds coconut cream + cashew paste = +140 cal. Low protein.
USDA: naan is ~290 cal per 90g piece. Restaurant naan varies 250-320 cal. That's 4-5 slices of regular bread in one piece. THE calorie trap — most people eat 2-3 pieces = 500-780 cal just in bread.
Plain naan + garlic butter brushed on top. Extra 40 cal from the butter/oil. Most popular naan at restaurants. Still a calorie trap.
Plain naan brushed with melted butter on both sides. Extra 60 cal from butter. Restaurants are generous with the butter.
Naan stuffed with melted cheese (paneer or mozzarella). The highest calorie naan. 130 cal more than plain naan. Tastes amazing, costs a lot of calories.

THE LEAN BREAD SWAP. Whole wheat, no yeast, dry-cooked on griddle. 110 cal vs 260 for naan = SAVE 150 cal per piece. Swap 2 naan (520 cal) for 2 roti (220 cal) = save 300 cal. Part of the lean Indian order.
Layered flatbread cooked with ghee/oil, often stuffed with potato or cauliflower. Similar calories to naan but more fat and less protein. Pan-fried = oily.
Small deep-fried puffed bread. 120 cal per piece but they're small — most people eat 3-4 = 360-480 cal. High fat ratio from deep frying. Deceptively caloric for the size.
USDA standard: basmati rice ~210 cal per cooked cup. Restaurant usually serves 1.5-2 cups = 315-420 cal. Plain rice is the lowest calorie base — just watch portion.

items.product_name: Biryani Rice (no meat, just rice portion)
items.notes: Basmati rice cooked with ghee, oil, saffron, fried onions. 70 cal MORE than plain rice per cup due to ghee/oil. This is just the rice from a biryani — meat adds more.
Rice cooked with whole spices, onions, some ghee. Between plain rice and biryani rice in calories. Fragrant but not as oily as biryani.
Basmati rice tempered with cumin seeds and a little ghee. Slightly less oil than pulao. Good middle-ground rice option.
Deep-fried pastry filled with spiced potato and peas. Standard restaurant samosa ~100g. Often served as a pair = 520 cal before the main course even arrives. Calorie trap appetizer.
Baked version saves ~80 cal per piece vs fried. Same filling, less oil. Not available at all restaurants — ask. 30% fewer calories than fried.
Chickpea-batter fried onion/vegetable fritters. Small but calorie-dense from deep frying. Restaurants serve 6-8 pieces per order = 660-880 cal for an appetizer plate. Very easy to mindlessly eat.
Thin lentil wafer, roasted or fried. Lowest calorie appetizer option. Good crunch fix. Even the fried version is only 55-70 cal because it's so thin. Safe snack while waiting.
THE LEAN APPETIZER. Boneless chicken marinated in yogurt/spices, grilled in tandoor. No sauce, no frying. 32g protein for 250 cal = excellent ratio. Best way to start an Indian meal if cutting.
Minced lamb/chicken shaped on skewers, grilled in tandoor. Good protein but higher fat than chicken tikka due to minced meat (often lamb). Still a solid appetizer choice — no frying.
Yogurt with cucumber/onion/spices. LOW CALORIE cooling side. Great with spicy food. Part of the lean Indian order. Full cup (240g) = ~160 cal. Use freely.
Pure sugar bomb. Mango + sugar cooked down. 11g sugar per tbsp. People dunk naan in this = bread + sugar. Small amounts OK but easy to overconsume because it's delicious.
THE FREE CONDIMENT. Mint + cilantro + green chili + lemon. Basically zero calories. Use this instead of mango chutney to save 45 cal per tbsp. Flavor without the cost.
Sweet-sour tamarind + jaggery/sugar. Similar to mango chutney — high sugar. Between mango chutney and mint chutney in calories. Use sparingly.
Pure fat — 120 cal per tbsp. Restaurants drizzle this on naan, rice, dal. Each drizzle = 60-120 cal you don't see. Ask for ghee on the side or skip it. Hidden calorie adder.

Yogurt + mango + sugar blended. 42g sugar = like drinking a soda + yogurt. The Indian restaurant 'dessert disguised as a drink.' If you're having one, skip dessert. Or order plain lassi (salted) for ~100 cal.
THE LASSI SWAP. Yogurt + water + salt/cumin. 170 cal LESS than mango lassi. Same probiotic benefit, no added sugar. Good with spicy food. The smart drink choice.
Black tea + whole milk + sugar + spices. Restaurant chai is SWEET — 14g sugar. Ask for 'less sugar' to cut to ~70 cal. Black tea chai (no milk/sugar) = ~5 cal.
Deep-fried milk dumplings soaked in sugar syrup. The most popular Indian dessert. Each piece is ~150 cal of pure fried dough + sugar. 34g sugar in 2 pieces.
Rice slow-cooked in sweetened milk with cardamom, nuts. Less calorie-dense than gulab jamun per serving. Still high sugar. Restaurant portions are usually generous.
Soft paneer dumplings in sweetened saffron milk. Lighter than gulab jamun (not fried). If you must have dessert, this is better than gulab jamun by ~50 cal.

lean_indian_order.description: The optimal fat-loss Indian restaurant order
lean_indian_order.notes: Compare to typical order: Butter chicken (490) + 2 garlic naan (600) + rice (210) + samosa (260) + mango lassi (280) = 1,840 cal, ~55g protein. The lean order saves ~930 cal with MORE protein.

top_swaps.savings: 120 cal per cup — no cream sauce
top_swaps.notes: Spicier but leaner. Tomato-vinegar base vs cream-butter base.

top_swaps.savings: 150 cal per piece — biggest single swap
top_swaps.notes: Whole wheat, no yeast, dry-cooked. Most impactful swap at an Indian restaurant.

top_swaps.savings: 120 cal per cup — spinach base vs cream-coconut
top_swaps.notes: More fiber, more iron, less fat. Still creamy-tasting.
Most people use 3-4 tbsp = 135-200 cal saved.
Same probiotic yogurt benefit, no added mango/sugar.

top_swaps.swap_to: Chicken Tikka appetizer (250 cal for 4 pcs)
top_swaps.notes: Grilled protein vs fried starch. Best appetizer swap.

top_swaps.savings: 70 cal per cup — skip the ghee-cooked rice
top_swaps.notes: Less flavorful but saves fat calories. Or just eat less rice.

top_swaps.savings: 120 cal per cup — no butter/cream finish
top_swaps.notes: Both are lentil-based but makhani = 'butter'. Tadka is the lean version.

hidden_calorie_traps: Ghee drizzled on naan/rice — each drizzle adds 60-120 cal invisibly
Naan basket — 2-3 pieces = 520-780 cal of bread before entree
Mango chutney — looks small but 50 cal/tbsp adds up fast with naan
Restaurant biryani — served as 2-3 cups = 700-1050 cal per plate
Pakora appetizer plate — 6-8 pieces = 660-880 cal of fried batter
Mango lassi — 280 cal drink with 42g sugar, basically dessert
Cream-based curries — butter chicken/korma/malai kofta all 450-520 cal/cup
Cheese naan — 390 cal per piece, almost a meal's worth of calories in bread
